coredata.dc:title: The photoluminescence of step-wise reduced graphene oxide quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yue
Zhang, Su
Shi, Liluo
Chen, Yaxin
Ma, Jing
Guo, Shasha
Chen, Xiaohong
Song, Huaihe
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) were synthesized by liquid-phase oxidation using the mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4. These GOQDs showed blue-green photoluminescence (PL), and a dual-wavelength emission. The emission peak could be distinguished as two peaks located at 463 nm and 496 nm. The GOQDs were further step-wise reduced using different amount of hydrazine to obtain reduced quantum dots (RQDs). The content and type of oxygen-containing functional groups varied with the reduction degree. For the same sample, the PL emission peaks exhibited independence on the excitation wavelength, revealing that the samples we prepared possessed a certain energy band structure. The PL of the GOQDs could be tailored through varying the reduction degree by changing the amount of hydrazine. The 463 nm emission peaks of the RQDs blue-shifted, while the 496 nm emission peaks red-shifted along with reduction degree. The PL intensities of the RQDs decreased drastically compared with that of the GOQDs. Based on these results, we proposed that the short wavelength emission peak was originated from oxygen-bearing groups, while the long wavelength emission peak was originated from sp2 domains of the GOQDs. Our work could help for the understanding of the PL behavior and mechanism of graphene-based quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence mechanism
Oxygen-containing groups
Size effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphologies of the GOQDs and the RQDs
The optical properties of GOQDs and RQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of the GOQDs (a, b), RQD-6 (c), RQD-25 (d), RQD-100 (e), RQD-200 (f).
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) Raman spectra of, (c) and (d) C1s spectra of GOQDs and RQDs.
The AFM images of GOQDs (a), RQD-6 (b), RQD-25 (c), RQD-100 (d) and RQD-200 (e), the spectra under each AFM images show the height profiles of the samples along the solid lines.

(a) Normalized UV-vis absorption, excitation spectra and emission spectra of GOQDs aqueous dispersion, (b) the normalized UV-vis absorption spectra of GOQDs, RQD-6, RQD-25, RQD-100 and RQD-200, (c) emission spectra of GOQDs aqueous dispersion under different excitation wavelength, (d) emission spectra of GOQDs and RQDs aqueous dispersion (the excitation wavelength is 330 nm).
Emission spectra of the GOQDs (a), RQD-3 (b), RQD-6 (c), RQD-12 (d), RQD-25 (e), RQD-50 (f), RQD-100 (g) and RQD-200 (h) aqueous dispersions under different λ ex.
Summery of components and C/O ratio in quantum dots.

row.entry: Contribution of components in C1s spectra (%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of PL properties of GQDs.

head.ce:title: The photoluminescence of step-wise reduced graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) were synthesized by liquid-phase oxidation using the mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4. These GOQDs showed blue-green photoluminescence (PL), and a dual-wavelength emission. The emission peak could be distinguished as two peaks located at 463 nm and 496 nm. The GOQDs were further step-wise reduced using different amount of hydrazine to obtain reduced quantum dots (RQDs). The content and type of oxygen-containing functional groups varied with the reduction degree. For the same sample, the PL emission peaks exhibited independence on the excitation wavelength, revealing that the samples we prepared possessed a certain energy band structure. The PL of the GOQDs could be tailored through varying the reduction degree by changing the amount of hydrazine. The 463 nm emission peaks of the RQDs blue-shifted, while the 496 nm emission peaks red-shifted along with reduction degree. The PL intensities of the RQDs decreased drastically compared with that of the GOQDs. Based on these results, we proposed that the short wavelength emission peak was originated from oxygen-bearing groups, while the long wavelength emission peak was originated from sp2 domains of the GOQDs. Our work could help for the understanding of the PL behavior and mechanism of graphene-based quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: The zero band gap feature of graphene greatly restricts its applications in many fields such as semiconductors, devices, and luminescent devices [1]. Researches about how to modulate the band gap of graphene have become a hotspot in recent years. One of the methods to modulate the band gap of graphene is cutting graphene up into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [2,3]. The typical size of quantum dots is under 10 nm [4,5], while graphene with the size below 100 nm also possesses strong quantum confinement effect and boundary effect [5–7]. The quantum size effect makes the electronic structure of GQDs transfer from continuous energy level to quasi splitting energy level, thus introduce energy band into the material [8].

As an important derivation of graphene, GQDs have attracted great attention in recent years due to their low cell toxicity, water solubility, chemical inertness and photoluminescence (PL) characteristics [4,5,10–12]. GQDs hold great potential in many fields such as photocatalysis [13], solar cell [14], biomedical imaging [15–17], ion detection [18]. Although GQDs possess many advantages, there are still some barriers which hinder its practical applications. One of the most difficult problems is that the PL mechanism of GQDs is still controversial because of their complex structure and non-stoichiometry composition [5,19]. This difficult problem may limit the further exploitation and application of GQDs.

The PL mechanisms of graphene-based PL materials have been widely studied in previous works. And researchers have put forward several opinions at present according to their experimental and/or simulation results. Typically, Jin et al. [20] prepared the GQDs and the amine group-functionalized GQDs from graphite oxide through a two-step process contained the oxidation in a mixed acid and hydrazine reduction. They demonstrated that the charge transfer between the amine group and the GQDs could tune the band gap of the GQDs. The PL properties of these GQDs could be attributed to the surface functional groups effect. Yang and co-workers [21] developed the GQDs from graphite oxide by a ozonation pre-oxide and hydrothermal deoxidization process, and then studied the influence of pH on the PL properties during the preparation process. They found that the oxygen-bearing groups on the basal planes and/or the edge of the GQDs [20,22–24] and the PL properties were influenced by the pH of the aqueous systems. They demonstrated that the pH of solution and oxygen-containing groups affected the PL properties significantly. Zhu et al. [25] prepared four kinds of GQDs (C222H42, C132H34, C96H30, C42H18) through organic routes [26,27], and investigated the PL mechanism of the samples by ultrafast spectroscopy [28]. They found the emission peaks of both C96 and C42 showed independence on the excitation wavelength, while the PL of C222 and C132 was too weak to be investigated by exiting techniques. They proposed that in these synthesized GQDs, the green PL depended not only on the size, but also on the edge state of the quantum dots. The energy gap between intrinsic state and edge state of the GQDs determined their optical properties. Kim's group [29] prepared the GQDs with the size ranged from 5 to 35 nm by hydrothermal cutting of graphene sheets. By examining their morphologies and structures with high-resolution transmission electron microscope and atomic force microscope, they deduced the PL behaviors were attributed to the size-dependent shape and the corresponding edge-state variations of the GQDs. Qiao et al. [30] oxidized commercially activated carbon through chemical routes to obtain multicolor fluorescent carbon dots. The size of prepared carbon dots ranged from 4 nm to 50 nm. They found that the larger particles were less luminescent than the smaller carbon dots with similar morphologies and surface passivation. They primarily deduced that different size and trap sites distribution in the samples were responsible for the multicolor PL phenomenon of these carbon dots. However, Xu et al. [31] proposed opposite thought according to their work. They prepared GQDs by a hydrothermal cutting route without any further dialysis treatment, and then performed single-particle spectroscopic measurement on the individual GQDs for the first time. They discovered that the PL emissions of individual GQDs showed size-independent properties. The lifetimes of the GQDs were strongly influenced by the number of layers of GQDs. They concluded that the PL of individual GQDs basically had nothing to do with their size.

Above all, although there are already some viewpoints at present, they are not suitable for all situations. Some of them seem not reasonable, even contradict mutually. In fact, GQDs obtained through different preparation methods may have different size, shape, functional groups distribution and edge states [20,22–24,32–38]. These complex factors cause great challenges to the accurate explanation of the PL mechanism of the GQDs.

In our work, we adopted liquid-phase oxidation method to prepare fluorescent GQDs from graphite oxide using an acid mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 as oxidizing agent. Through the severe chemical oxidation process, the quantum dots were decorated by large amount of oxygen-bearing functional groups [20,22–24]. So we named the quantum dots we prepared in this work as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). The GOQDs were further step-wise reduced by hydrazine to prepare reduced quantum dots (RQDs), with the amount of hydrazine ranged from 3 μL to 200 μL. We discovered that the emission peaks of the GOQDs could be distinguished as two peaks located at 463 nm and 496 nm. All the samples exhibited excitation-independent properties although their sizes were not uniform. Besides, for the RQDs with varying reduction degrees, we found that the two emission peaks showed different shift trend. These changes were further explained by a series of optical properties characterization of the GOQDs and the RQDs aqueous solutions.

First, graphite oxide was synthesized by modified Hummers' method [39,40]. For the preparation of the GOQDs, 2 g graphite oxide powder was put into a 500 ml three-neck flask, then a mixture acid of 90 ml H2SO4 (98%, wt. %) and 30 ml HNO3 (38%, wt. %) was added into the flask. The system were kept in 85 °C water bath under reflux for 48 h. After the reaction terminated, the product was diluted into water and separated by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 15 min). The supernatant was then dialysis to neutral (7000 Da molecular weight interception) to get GOQDs (see Fig. 1 ).

To prepare RQDs, 50 ml GOQDs solution (ca. 5 mg ml−1) was added into a beaker. After adding 3 μL, 6 μL, 12 μL, 25 μL, 50 μL, 100 μL and 200 μL hydrazine solution (80%, wt. %) respectively and kept in 50 °C water bath for 12 h under stirring, RQDs with different reduction degree were obtained. After cooling to room temperature, the RQDs were further treated to neutral by dialysis (7000 Da molecular weight interception). The RQDs were marked as RQD-3, RQD-6, RQD-12, RQD-25, RQD-50, RQD-100 and RQD-200 respectively according to the dosage of hydrazine.

The morphologies of the samples were characterized by HRTEM (FEI-T20). Each of the samples was fully dispersed in ethanol and then dropped on a micro-grid carbon supported film, then dried naturally. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured by Nicolet iS50 infrared spectroscopy instrument. Raman spectroscopy was carried out using a 514 nm laser (Aramis, Jobin Yvon). Each of the samples was dispersed in ethanol, and let it dripped into SiO2/Si substrates and dried naturally. For each samples 3 different points were measured. The element content and bonding state was characterized by X-ray photoelectric spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250) using Al X-ray source with 200 eV pass energy for survey and 30 eV for high resolution scans. The surface morphology was tested by aromatic force microscope (AFM, DMFASTSCAN2-SYS, Germany). The UV-vis spectroscopy was performed on UV-2450 ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The fluorescent spectra were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The samples for photoluminescent characterization were the GOQDs or the RQDs aqueous dispersions with the absolute UV-vis adsorption adjusted to 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphologies of the GOQDs and the RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 gives the HRTEM images of GOQDs. The GOQDs show irregular circle, with the average size of GOQDs of ca. 10 nm. From the high-resolution image of an individual GOQDs showed in the inset of 1b, the lattice structure in the GOQDs is obvious, indicating the π-π conjugation and sp2 bonded networks of graphene sheets [32]. Besides, the direction of the lattice structure is multiple, which means the GOQDs are a kind of polycrystalline material. The average size of RQDs is still ca. 10 nm, because the oxygen-containing groups on the basal plane and/or edge of GOQDs were removed during the reduction process, the size of the quantum dots was not changed.

Fig. 3 a displays the FT-IR spectra of the GOQDs and the RQDs. The absorption peaks at 1240 cm−1, 1592 cm−1, 1720 cm−1 are attributed to C-O-C, C=C, -C=O, respectively. The broad peak between 2640 and 3680 cm−1 corresponds to -COOH/-OH [37]. In the original GOQDs, there are various functional groups such as –COOH, -C=O, -OH, -C-O-C [41]. With the increase of reduction degree, the amounts of –COOH and -OH groups decrease gradually, while C-O-C and –C=O still exists. This meant –COOH and -OH are not stable and most of them were removed during reduction process, while the relative stable -C=O and C-O-C still preserved [42,43].

Fig. 3b gives the Raman spectra of GOQDs and RQDs. The peaks at ca. 1361 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 correspond D-band and G-band respectively in quantum dots [43]. The D-band represents the defect in graphitic structure [44], and G-band represents the symmetric vibration of carbon atoms in graphite structure [45]. The intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) represents the defect density in materials [43,46]. The broad D-band and strong G-band indicates the GOQDs exhibit much defective structure. With the increase of reduction degree, the position of D-band and G-band do not move, and the image feature remains almost unchanged, only the intensity of D-band shows slight decrement. This meant that the defect structure in the quantum dots were restored to some extent through reduction. The most common position of G-band of graphitic materials locates at ca. 1580 cm−1. The G-band of the quantum dots is blue-shifted, which might be attributed to the defect structure caused by impurity atoms and edge structures [46–49].

Interestingly, the value of ID/IG is not linearly related to the reduction degree. It increases at first and decreases later. The maximum value of ID/IG is 0.971, when the amount of hydrazine is 6 μL. We primarily assumed the evolution process of the RQDs' surface structure. Oxygen-containing functional groups distribute on the surface of the GOQDs randomly. These decorated groups destroy the sp2 structure of graphene flakes and form sp3 clusters [50,51]. So the value of ID/IG is high (ID/IG = 0.886). When the reduction degree is low, a part of groups are removed. This made the local sp3 carbon atoms convert back into sp2 carbon atoms. However, it is noteworthy that the size of sp2 clusters is not changed, but the number of sp2 clusters increase during the primary stage [26,40]. Therefore, the value of ID/IG increases. When the amount of hydrazine continues to increase to above 6 μL, part of sp2 clusters join together and become contiguous, which make the value of ID/IG decrease afterwards.

In the C1s XPS spectra, the peak located at ca. 284.8 eV is ascribed to the C=C and/or C-C bond in graphitic conjugated area [52]. For the GOQDs, there exist various oxygen-containing groups. The peaks located at ca. 286.5 eV and ca. 288.5 eV represent the carbon atoms connected with C-O and C=O, respectively [52]. During the reduction process, the intensity of C-O and C=O peaks decrease, and the phenomenon is consistent with the FT-IR spectra. This trend confirmed that the oxygen-containing functional groups change obviously during the reduction process [28]. The components of the products and the contribution of various carbon atoms measured by XPS are summarized in Table 1 . The small amount of nitrogen atoms in the RQDs are attributed to the hydrazine we used during the reduction of GOQDs. After reduction, the atomic ratio of carbon and oxygen and the fraction of C=C/C-C increases obviously, while the content of C-O and C=O decreases. Actually, it is these decorated oxygen-containing functional groups endow the quantum dots the good solubility in water.

The size of GOQDs and RQD is about 10 nm, which is in accordance with the results of HRTEM. And the height of GOQDs is about 3.85 nm, which is the thickness of about 3 layers of graphene oxide (see Fig. 4 ). This value decreases after reduction, which is ascribed to the removal of oxygen-containing groups. Because the layer distance decrease after the groups are removed and the layers restack tightly. The size of GOQDs and RQD is about 10 nm, which is also in accordance with the results from HRTEM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of GOQDs and RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 gives the optical properties of the GOQDs aqueous dispersions. The typical ultraviolet absorption peak of the GOQDs emerges at 230 nm, which is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [53–56], and extends to the visible light range. This characteristic peak does not move after reduction. This is unusual because this absorption peak red-shift after reduction in graphene oxide. And the reason for this unusual phenomenon is that the size of GOQDs is too small. Even though all the oxygen-containing groups are removed and all the sp3 carbons are converted back to sp2 carbon, the variation of conjugation degree in GOQDs is still too small to cause obvious shift of absorption peak.

The maximum excitation peak is located at 277 nm. Similar to Zhang's [57] results, the GOQDs show a dual-wavelength emission. The emission peak of the GOQDs we prepared can be distinguished as two peaks locate at 463 nm (Peak 1) and 496 nm (Peak 2). These two peaks are basically independent on the excitation wavelength (λ ex) and remain at ca. 463 nm and 496 nm, respectively. From Fig. 5d given above, with the increase of reduction degree, the fluorescent intensity of quantum dots is drastically decreased. It is noteworthy that, the absolute UV-vis absorption of all the samples we used for the detection equals to 1. So the PL intensities of the samples are comparable. Combined the FT-IR and XPS analysis results, this trend seems to confirm that the oxygen-bearing groups indeed hold a great influence on the PL properties of quantum dots [10,53]. As the λ ex equals to 330 nm, the Peak 1 of the RQDs are slightly blue shift compared to that of the GOQDs, while Peak 2 red shift. We deduced that there might not only one factor that influence the PL properties of quantum dots [24,35]. The two peaks might be controlled by different factors.

Fig. 6 shows the PL emission spectra of the quantum dots under various λ ex from 260 nm to 390 nm. During the reduction process, the Peak 1 blue shifts gradually from ca. 463 nm to ca. 454 nm. In the meantime, Peak 2 red-shifts to above 500 nm at first, then returns to 496 nm. This phenomenon is in good agreement with the result in Fig. 3d. In conclusion, 1) the emission peak shifts obviously during reduction process, which means the peak is sensitive towards the oxygen-bearing functional groups and 2) the emission wavelength can be modulated by controlled reduction.

According to the quantum confinement effect, different-sized nanoparticles have different band gap. The larger the size of nanoparticles, the smaller the band gap is. The red-shift of Peak 2 during reduction process might be attributed to the optical selection of different-sized sp2 clusters [20,25,29,30,58]. Small sp2 aromatic domains with certain sizes possess suitable energy gap, which would be responsible for the PL of the GOQDs. When the reduction degree enhances, although there are new sp2 domains formed, the average size of sp2 clusters tends to become bigger [40]. Therefore the band gap become smaller. This explained the red shift of Peak 2 from ca. 490 nm to above 500 nm. But when the reduction degree is high enough, sp2 domains with suitable size grew too big to provide the PL properties [59], while those newly formed small sp2 started to play as the fluorophores for the PL. This made Peak 2 converted back to ca. 496 nm. So the Peak 2 might be originated from certain-sized sp2 domains on the surface of the GOQDs.

The certain size of sp2 conjugated domains is hard to be measured or calculated accurately because the GOQDs have complex structure and non-stoichiometry composition. But the bang gap of GQDs which consists of 13 aromatic rings is 2.508 eV [20]. This value is similar to the experimental data for the long wavelength PL peak of GOQDs we prepared (2.502 eV). The diameter of 13 aromatic rings is about 6.5 nm, and the size of GOQDs we prepared is no bigger than 15 nm.

It is impossible that all the oxygen-containing groups are removed and there are no sp3 carbons. In fact, there are still about 17% oxygen atoms even the amount of hydrazine is as high as 100 μL (summarized in Table 1). So there must exists lots of sp3 carbons separated by sp2 domains, thus the size of these sp2 domains is definitely smaller that the whole size of the GOQDs. Therefore, we can primarily deduced that this “certain size” is among 6.5 nm–15 nm.

Actually, the decorated oxygen-containing groups have significant effect on the optical properties. Li et al. [60] had calculated that the ground state energy gap reduced in the edge-functionalized GQDs. As the fact that groups such as –COOH, -OH and –C=O always existed at the edge of the GQDs instead of the basal plane [40], these groups could evidently change the energy gap of GQDs. During the reduction process, unstable groups such as –COOH, -OH and –C=O were removed primarily. So the energy gap of the GOQDs increased correspondingly. The Peak 1 exhibited a slight blue-shift of ca. 9 nm compared with that of the GOQDs after fully reduction. In other words, Peak 1 was originated from the oxygen-bearing groups. Interestingly, what did not change of these RQDs was that all the emission peaks were independent on the λ ex. This implied that the RQDs still had stable band structure and the quantum dots with the similar size could possess different PL properties because of the different decorated functional groups [6,40].

Above all, although the size of quantum dots is not so uniform, the emission peaks are all independent on the λ ex, which means the quantum dots we prepared exhibit certain energy band structure. With the increase of reduction degree, the PL intensities of these quantum dots decrease drastically. This demonstrates that oxygen-bearing groups have significant influence on the fluorescent properties of quantum dots. However, the change of quantity, type and distribution of oxygen-containing functional groups during reduction process is very complicate and cannot be predicted quantitatively. Therefore, the explicit relationship between functional groups and fluorescent properties is not clear.

Besides, from Fig. 6, the emission wavelength of quantum dots can be modulated by controlling the amount of reductant. So there is a possibility that we can prepare quantum dots with different colors. The color of most GQDs that have been reported is green (Table 2 ). The autofluorescence of most cells is also green, resulting the green fluorescent labeling materials may not be suitable on certain occasions. As the fluorescent color can be regulated, we can use quantum dots with different colors for high contrast biosensing and bioimaging applications. Taking advantage of the color adjustability of the GQDs, we can greatly expand the application fields of quantum dots.

In this work, we prepared blue-green fluorescent GOQDs by liquid-phase oxidation method. We step-wise reduced the materials with hydrazine to further study the PL mechanism. Our experiment results revealed that the PL mechanism of GOQDs could be attributed to the combination of size effect and surface functional groups, especially –COOH and –OH groups. To say exactly, the sp2 conjugated domains with certain size on the GOQDs surface influence the long wavelength emission, and the functional groups affect the short wavelength emission of the GOQDs. Our work can help for the understanding the PL behaviors and mechanism and advancing the design of graphene-based quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51202009 and 51272019).
